author: @kvishnu2416
text: Hi Robert I've been doing some research on graphene of late as a conductive ink, I've read from a different source that when graphene ink is used binder free it has good conductive properties so I've used graphene ink with the carrier as IPA,  with a solid content of ink ~14% but the problem is it isn't viscous enough to use it for printing as it is binder free. Is there some way around this problem. I was thinking about screen printing it on a paper( normal paper and a glossy paper) , I did try it out in an inkjet cartridge but obviously the sponge in the cartridge is going to act as a filter and separate out the  graphene from the carrier and eventually be no use . So I thought of sticking with screen printing but going binder free as my further part of the experiment needs a high conductive ink also I want to avoid annealing . Please suggest me as to what changes I should do ?  Your suggestion would be of great help !!!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: all i can think of is adding a thickening agent like cmc or agar-agar

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes of course it would - as to the rest - i have no idea

replies.author: @kvishnu2416
replies.text: Thank you sir :) , BTW your videos are great .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - sorry i couldn't be more help

author: @everlasting1154
text: I made the bare conductive ink but ran into a problem. Although it stuck to the marble tile I am using do my heater it refused to stick to the copper tape. I added pba glue to the formula and got the ink to stick but the resistance jumped.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try cleaning the tape with IPA or acetone first and lightly abrading the surface

author: @everlasting1154

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is an ink making playlist with some of the basics and lots of making graphene vids - uf you put the two together you should be there. It depends on what you want your ink to do for choice of binder. PVA is valid but not very abrasive or water resistant.

author: @DustinjBailey
text: How does the conductivity compare between I thin line drawn with a pen vs. a thin copper wire of equal length and cross section area?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: about how you would expect - the copper is a million times more conductive

replies.author: @DustinjBailey
replies.text:
Is the same true for the sheets you produce, if they were to be cut into wire sized pieces?

I love your videos by the way, very educational and inspiring. Keep them coming :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - would you be looking for a fake wire by any chance?

replies.author: @DustinjBailey
replies.text: You've got me.  I have an idea for a flat generator, about 1/4" thick.  Just thinking of conceptual options at this point, and I was hoping your film would do the trick, because if I laser cut the sheet, I could get the exact shape I need.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: have a look at the stuff on flat transformers and skin effects in conductors - that is going to be really important for you - but if you are looking at cutting just use copper foil

author: @m3sca1
text:
Another application for these products is RF shield paint for metal detector coils.
Mylar and also graphite paint are 2 products that have employed as shielding for the coils.
Graphene would be superior because less would be required and it would be thinner as a result. That would reduce the weight of the coil, and also the amount of energy lost in the sheild when the pulse goes through it.

author: @m3sca1
text: I wonder if applying a voltage to the ink during the drying process would improve conductivity readings. My thoughts are that it would help align the sheet edges.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: definitely worth a try mate

author: @samfosteriam
text: Any conductive adhesives in the works? I've been trying a few and they are either too expensive (for me to even try, failed to score a sample) or not very conductive, and/or not very adhesive. Pretty poor state of affairs in general - for the hobbyist at least. I'm particularly interested in a low/room temp cure.
